content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6441                    322-6444

May 13, 1982


Mr. David M. Kennedy
Office of the City Attorney
200 Lincoln Avenue
Salinas, CA  93901

Dear Mr. Kennedy:

I am writing in response to your letter concerning
Schedule D of Form 730 (Statement of Economic Interests).
As you correctly stated in your memorandum to the mayor and
the city council of the City of Salinas, Section 82030(a)1/
of the Political Reform Act requires a filer owning a 10
percent or greater interest in a business entity to list the
business entity on Schedule D of his or her Statement and to
disclose (with general categories) his or her pro rata share
of gross receipts received by the business. This is true
even if the business has incurred a net loss. Your inquiry
is as to the reasoning behind this rule.

Section 82030(a) provides that:

...Income of an individual also includes a pro rata
share of any income of any business entity or trust in
which the individual owns, directly or beneficially, a
10 percent interest or greater....

It has been the Commission's position that this reference in
Section 82030(a) refers to gross, rather than net, income.2/
This interpretation is consistent with the Act's provisions
regarding disclosure of clients of business entities. (Section

1/ Hereinafter all references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

2/ See the enclosed copy of a letter written to Anthony
Alperin discussing past advice letters on this subject.

Mr. David M. Kennedy
May 13, 1982
Page Two


87207(b)(3)).3/ That section states that the determination
of whether or not a filer must disclose a client's name
depends on the amount of the gross receipts from the client.

There are also several policy reasons behind the Commission's
interpretation of Section 82030(a). First, gross receipts
are easily and objectively determinable, whereas the amount
of net income may vary with the accounting methods used and
the tax deductions taken by a filer. Second, in situations
where the business entity suffers a net loss, a "net receipts"
test would allow an owner-filer to leave undisclosed substantial
sources of income to the business.

If a filer is concerned that, by reporting gross income
on Schedule D, he or she will give an erroneous impression,
in situations where their business experienced a net loss,
the filer may make a supplemental note on Schedule D indicating
the net loss.

I hope this letter has been helpful to you. If you
have any additional questions, please feel free to contact
me at 916/322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:km


_______________________

3/ Section 87207(b)(3) states: "When income of a
business entity, including income of a sole proprietorship,
is required to be reported under this article, the statement
shall contain:

(3) ...the name of every person from whom the
business entity received payments if the filer's pro
rata share of gross receipts from such person was equal
to or greater than $10,000 during a calendar year."

City of Salinas
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY • 200 Lincoln Avenue • Salinas, California 93901 • (408) 758-7256

April 6, 1982


Alice Hudson
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Dear Ms. Hudson:

Pursuant to my phone conversation with you yesterday, I have
prepared the attached Memorandum to the City Council.

Please be so kind as to advise whether my memorandum correctly
states the position of the F.P.P.C. and, if so, and if you
know, the reasoning behind a reporting requirement such as
this when there is a net loss.  Such a reporting requirement
certainly can give a gross misleading impression as to the
worth of an individual, often to that person's political
detriment.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

DAVID M. KENNEDY
City Attorney

DMK:lt

Attachment

INFORMATION

MEMORANDUM

April 6, 1982


TO:          HONORABLE MAYOR AND CITY COUNCIL

FROM:        CITY ATTORNEY

SUBJECT:     STATEMENTS OF ECONOMIC INTEREST

A question has arisen regarding reporting requirements for your
Statement of Economic Interest the answer to which may be applic-
able to one or more members of the Council.

The facts are that a Councilman owns an interest of 10 percent or
more in a business entity which has no net income and from which
the Councilman receives no income at all. The question is whether
this business enterprise must be shown on Schedule D "Income."

The answer is that the business entity must be listed on Schedule
D and the person reporting must show his pro-rata share of gross
receipts received by the business.

Example, you own 1/4 of a business with gross receipts of $80,000
which has expenditures of $100,000, or a net loss of $20,000.
Even though your actual pro-rata share of the business was a
$5,000 loss, and you received nothing, you must mark the box
entitled:

     "Amount or value received was greater than $10,000."

Income of an individual includes, in part, "a pro-rata share of
any income of any business entity...in which the individual or
spouse owns, directly or indirectly...a 10 percent interest or
greater." (Emphasis added) Gov. Code §82030[a]).

Seizing on the words "any income" in Section 82030[a], the FPPC, in
the instructions to Schedule D, requires the reporting of "your
pro-rata share of gross receipts of a business entity..."etc.

The foregoing has been confirmed by phone with the Staff of the
F.P.P.C.

[signature]
DAVID M. KENNEDY
City Attorney

DMK:lt

cc:  City Clerk
     City Manager

sections.question_synthetic: Must a person owning 10% or greater interest in a business entity with a net loss still disclose their pro rata share of gross receipts on Schedule D of Form 730, and what is the reasoning for this requirement?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, Section 82030(a) requires disclosure of pro rata share of gross receipts regardless of net loss, and the FPPC interprets this to mean gross income rather than net income. This interpretation is based on the objective determinability of gross receipts and prevents owners from hiding substantial income sources when businesses report losses.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request (April 6, 1982 letter from City Attorney) followed by FPPC response (May 13, 1982 letter from Counsel). No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections; extracted synthesis from letter body. Includes attached memorandum providing context. References to Government Code sections provided in footnotes.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must a person owning 10% or greater interest in a business entity with a net loss still disclose their pro rata share of gross receipts on Schedule D of Form 730, and what is the reasoning for this requirement?

CONCLUSION: Yes, Section 82030(a) requires disclosure of pro rata share of gross receipts regardless of net loss, and the FPPC interprets this to mean gross income rather than net income. This interpretation is based on the objective determinability of gross receipts and prevents owners from hiding substantial income sources when businesses report losses.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel responds to City Attorney inquiry regarding Schedule D reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act, explaining that filers with 10% or greater interest in business entities must disclose pro rata share of gross receipts even if the business has incurred a net loss.
